_comment: Doneness rules. Temperatures are USDA safe MINIMUM internal temperatures, sourced and cited per rule. Do NOT edit a pull_temp_f without updating its source. Never let these be model-generated (see ADR 0002, PRD §10).

rules.label: Chicken (any cut)
rules.visual_cue: Juices run clear; meat is opaque white to the bone with no pink near the joint.
rules.no_thermometer_cue: Cut into the thickest part: the flesh should be white/opaque all the way through with clear (not pink or red) juices. If in doubt, cook longer — undercooked chicken is unsafe.
rules.safety_note: Poultry must reach 165°F throughout. There is no safe rare for chicken. When the rule and what you see disagree, the rule wins — keep cooking.

source.name: USDA FSIS — Safe Minimum Internal Temperature Chart

rules.label: Ground beef, pork, lamb
rules.visual_cue: No pink remains and juices run clear.
rules.no_thermometer_cue: Break a piece open at the center — color should be uniform brown/gray with no pink. Color alone is unreliable; a thermometer is strongly preferred for ground meat.
rules.safety_note: Ground meats need 160°F because grinding spreads surface bacteria throughout.

rules.label: Beef, pork, lamb, veal (steaks, chops, roasts)
rules.visual_cue: Firm with a little give; interior reaches the target before resting.
rules.no_thermometer_cue: Whole cuts are hard to judge safely by eye — use a thermometer. Rest 3 minutes after pulling so the temperature evens out.
rules.safety_note: 145°F THEN a 3-minute rest is the safe minimum for whole cuts. Higher temps give more well-done results.

source.name: USDA FSIS — Safe Minimum Internal Temperature Chart

rules.label: Fish (finfish)
rules.visual_cue: Flesh is opaque and flakes/separates easily with a fork.
rules.no_thermometer_cue: Press gently and twist a fork in the thickest part — done fish turns opaque and flakes apart cleanly instead of looking translucent and resisting.

rules.label: Shrimp, lobster, crab, scallops
rules.visual_cue: Flesh is pearly/white and opaque; shrimp curl into a loose C (a tight O means overcooked).
rules.no_thermometer_cue: The official test is visual, not a temperature: cook until the flesh is pearly and opaque. Shrimp go from grey/translucent to pink-and-white and curl into a loose C.
rules.safety_note: FoodSafety.gov specifies a visual test for shellfish rather than a single internal temperature.

rules.label: Eggs & egg dishes
rules.visual_cue: Whites and yolk are firm (or the dish is set in the center, not runny).
rules.no_thermometer_cue: Cook eggs until both white and yolk are firm; cook egg dishes (frittata, egg bites) until the center is set and a knife comes out clean.

rules.label: Leftovers & casseroles
rules.visual_cue: Steaming hot all the way through.
rules.no_thermometer_cue: Reheat until steaming hot throughout, stirring so there are no cold spots.
